Daily DAP Accessibility Report — 2026-03-26#

Run ID: run-2026-03-26-8492f8df28 · Status: success · Source data: 2026-03-24 · Generated: 2026-03-26T09:40:07.465Z

About These Reports#

The Digital Analytics Program (DAP) is a U.S. government analytics service that collects website traffic data across participating federal agencies. DAP tracks page views, visitor counts, and usage patterns for hundreds of government websites, providing transparency into how the public engages with federal digital services.

This report measures the quality and accessibility of the top 100 most-visited U.S. government URLs as reported by DAP. Each day, Lighthouse scans are run against these URLs to measure:

  • Performance - How fast pages load for users (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • Accessibility - How well pages work for users with disabilities, following WCAG guidelines (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • Best Practices - Whether pages follow modern web development standards (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • SEO - How well pages are optimized for search engines (scores 0-100, higher is better)

Accessibility findings come from axe-core, the industry-standard accessibility testing engine embedded in Lighthouse. The axe findings surface specific WCAG violations such as missing alternative text, insufficient color contrast, and missing form labels that make government websites harder to use for people with disabilities.

Traffic data reflects daily visitor counts from DAP. URLs are ranked by page load count, ensuring the most-used government pages are prioritized for quality measurement.

Accessibility Trend Narrative#

Over the past 28 days of data (2026-02-26 to 2026-03-25), government website accessibility scores have improved (+24.48 points, from 68 to 92.48). Performance scores have improved (+18.56 points, from 38 to 56.56).

Today's aggregate accessibility score of 91.49 reflects the mean Lighthouse accessibility score across 100 successfully scanned government URLs. A score above 90 indicates generally strong compliance with WCAG automated checks, though manual testing is always recommended to fully assess accessibility.

Common Accessibility Issues (Top 10)#

The following axe-core rules were most frequently violated across scanned URLs today. These patterns indicate systemic accessibility barriers present across multiple government websites.

Human impact narratives for top issues

The following narratives describe the real-world impact of each accessibility barrier on people with disabilities. These barriers are not abstract technical failures; they prevent citizens from independently accessing government services.

target-size: Digital Motor Access

Small touch targets act as a digital gatekeeper, excluding individuals with tremors, arthritis, or limited dexterity from accessing essential services independently. These technical failures transform a routine interaction into a source of failure, stripping away the autonomy of citizens who require a frictionless, accessible interface to participate in digital life. The approximately 58 million Americans with ambulatory or self-care disabilities are disproportionately impacted by inadequate touch target sizing on government mobile websites.

Affected groups:

  • People with Parkinson's disease, arthritis, or hand tremors
  • Older adults with reduced fine motor control
  • People with motor disabilities using alternative pointing devices
  • People in situational impairment contexts (e.g., commuting, holding a child)

heading-order: Document Structure Navigation

Screen reader users navigate complex government websites primarily through heading structure, using headings as a table of contents to jump between sections. Skipped heading levels break the logical document outline, causing confusion about the hierarchy of information and forcing users to re-read sections to understand the relationship between topics, adding significant time and effort to information-gathering tasks.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

landmark-one-main: Primary Content Identification

Without a main landmark, screen reader users lose their most efficient way to skip to the primary content of a government page. Every page visit requires tabbing through the entire navigation structure to reach the actual content, a significant time and effort burden that accumulates across repeated visits to government websites for citizens managing ongoing benefit cases or legal matters.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

color-contrast: Visual Information Access

Low contrast text is one of the most pervasive barriers on government websites, rendering critical information invisible to the approximately 26 million Americans with low vision or color blindness. When agency announcements, form instructions, error messages, or legal notices are displayed in insufficient contrast, affected citizens are denied equal access to the information they need to exercise their rights and access public services.

Affected groups:

  • People with low vision including age-related vision loss
  • People who are color blind (approximately 8% of men, 0.5% of women)

label-content-name-mismatch: Voice Control Activation

When the accessible name of an element does not match its visible text, voice control users cannot activate it using the words they see on screen. Saying "click Submit" to a button whose visual label is "Submit" but whose accessible name is "submitbtn" results in no action. This mismatch silently breaks voice control for citizens with motor disabilities who depend on this input method to interact with government websites.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People with low vision relying on screen magnifiers
  • People who are deaf using keyboard navigation
  • People with motor disabilities using voice control software

link-name: Link Purpose Clarity

Links without accessible names are completely useless to screen reader users who navigate government pages by jumping between links. An unnamed link could lead anywhere, and activating it unknowingly could trigger downloads, open unexpected pages, or initiate unintended processes. On government websites, unnamed links undermine the informed consent principle by preventing citizens from knowing where a link will take them.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

image-alt: Visual Content Access

Images without alternative text are completely invisible to blind users and to search engines. On government websites, informational images, diagrams, infographics, and document screenshots without alt text deprive blind citizens of the same information available to sighted visitors. Critical details like emergency instructions, benefit eligibility charts, or form procedure diagrams can be entirely inaccessible, putting these citizens at a disadvantage in accessing government services and information.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools

link-in-text-block: Color-Independent Link Identification

When links are distinguished only by color, people with color vision deficiency cannot identify them as links and may miss critical references to additional information, forms, or resources. On government websites, overlooked links in informational content can mean missing essential instructions, deadlines, or procedures that affect a citizen's ability to access services or comply with requirements.

Affected groups:

  • People with low vision who may not perceive color differences
  • People who are color blind (approximately 8% of men, 0.5% of women)

aria-allowed-attr: Assistive Technology Reliability

Invalid ARIA attributes cause assistive technologies to misinterpret or malfunction on interactive controls. For screen reader users navigating complex government forms or applications, broken ARIA creates unpredictable behavior that can make tasks impossible to complete, denying access to essential services.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text assistive tools
  • People with motor disabilities using switch access or voice control

html-has-lang: Language Identification

Without a language attribute, screen readers default to the user's system language settings, which may cause government content to be read in the wrong language with incorrect pronunciation. This is particularly harmful for multilingual citizens accessing government services in their non-primary language, and for users of braille displays that depend on language metadata for proper rendering.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with cognitive or language differences
Disability icon key
Without Vision — People who are blind or have no functional vision (1.0% of U.S. population — ~3,400,000 Americans)
Limited Vision — People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast (2.4% of U.S. population — ~8,100,000 Americans)
Without Perception of Color — People who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness) (4.3% of U.S. population — ~14,500,000 Americans)
Without Hearing — People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content (0.3% of U.S. population — ~1,100,000 Americans)
Limited Hearing — People with hearing loss who may struggle with audio without accommodations (3.5% of U.S. population — ~11,900,000 Americans)
Without Speech — People who cannot use speech or voice-based input effectively (0.5% of U.S. population — ~1,700,000 Americans)
Limited Manipulation — People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity (2.2% of U.S. population — ~7,600,000 Americans)
Limited Reach and Strength — People with limited reach, strength, or stamina (5.8% of U.S. population — ~19,600,000 Americans)
Limited Language, Cognitive, and Learning Abilities — People with cognitive, learning, or language differences (4.7% of U.S. population — ~15,900,000 Americans)

These icons show which groups of people with disabilities are excluded by each accessibility barrier. Where page view data is available, each icon shows an estimated number of people potentially excluded (page loads for affected URLs × disability prevalence rate from U.S. Census ACS 2023). Hover over or focus an icon to see the full estimate and methodology. Icons follow the Section 508 Functional Performance Criteria and the equivalent EU EN 301 549 v3.2.1 Table B.2 categories.

Prevalence data: U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 1-Year Estimates, Table B18101; supplemental data from CDC, NIDCD, AFB, and NIH/NEI. U.S. population base: ~336,500,000 (2023 estimate). Reviewed annually.

See the Section 508 WCAG to FPC mapping for additional detail on how accessibility requirements map to functional needs.

Usability Heuristics Summary#

The most common accessibility violations map to Nielsen Norman Group's 10 usability heuristics. This table shows which heuristics are most affected by today's top axe-core violations, helping to identify where systemic usability barriers exist.

NN/g usability heuristics affected by today's top accessibility violations
# Heuristic Issue patterns URL violations Related axe rules
6 Recognition rather than recall 4 80 heading-order, landmark-one-main, color-contrast, link-name
4 Consistency and standards 4 76 heading-order, landmark-one-main, label-content-name-mismatch, link-name
8 Aesthetic and minimalist design 4 66 landmark-one-main, color-contrast, image-alt, link-in-text-block
10 Help and documentation 4 66 landmark-one-main, color-contrast, image-alt, link-in-text-block
1 Visibility of system status 3 54 landmark-one-main, color-contrast, link-in-text-block
9 Help users recognize, diagnose, and recover from errors 3 54 landmark-one-main, color-contrast, link-in-text-block
7 Flexibility and efficiency of use 3 53 heading-order, label-content-name-mismatch, link-name
5 Error prevention 2 35 landmark-one-main, link-in-text-block

Americans Excluded by Disability Today#

Based on 66,043,930 page loads across successfully scanned government URLs and U.S. disability prevalence rates from the U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 1-Year Estimates, Table B18101 (2023), an estimated 4,810,000 Americans encountered an accessibility barrier on a government website today.

Each row shows the number of people in a disability group who visited a page with at least one accessibility issue that affects their group. Prevalence rates and U.S. population estimates are derived from the American Community Survey (ACS) and supplemental sources. These figures are rough estimates intended to illustrate the scale of accessibility barriers.

Estimated Americans excluded today by disability category (2023 Census data)
Icon Disability Group (Section 508 FPC) U.S. Prevalence U.S. Population Affected Page Loads with Barriers Est. Americans Excluded Today
Limited Reach and Strength 5.8% ~19,600,000 29,200,000 1,690,000
Limited Manipulation 2.2% ~7,600,000 43,400,000 950,000
Without Perception of Color 4.3% ~14,500,000 18,700,000 800,000
Limited Vision 2.4% ~8,100,000 24,000,000 570,000
Without Vision 1.0% ~3,400,000 42,900,000 420,000
Limited Language, Cognitive, and Learning Abilities 4.7% ~15,900,000 4,730,000 220,000
Without Hearing 0.3% ~1,100,000 42,900,000 120,000

Sources: U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 1-Year Estimates, Table B18101; disability prevalence from ACS 2023 and supplemental CDC/NIDCD/NIH data. Reviewed annually.

Performance Impact on Americans#

Google defines a good Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) as under 2.5 seconds and recommends pages under 1.6 MB. The figures below estimate how much extra time Americans spend waiting, and how much extra data is transferred, because government websites fall short of these benchmarks. Calculations are based on 96 successfully scanned URLs with Lighthouse timing data.

Estimated performance impact vs. Google benchmarks for government URLs scanned on March 26, 2026
Metric Estimated total (March 26, 2026) Notes
Extra time waiting (vs 2.5s LCP benchmark) 9 years, 10 months, 21 days 312,154,372 seconds
Extra data transferred (vs 1.6 MB page weight benchmark) ~51.7 TB (2,151 copies of Wikipedia) Across 96 URLs with weight data

Extra time is calculated as: for each scanned URL, max(0, actual LCP − 2.5s) × page loads. Extra data is calculated as: max(0, actual page weight − 1.6 MB) × page loads. LCP and page weight are measured by Lighthouse. Wikipedia copy count uses a size of 24.05 GB per Wikipedia:Size of Wikipedia. These are rough estimates based on a sample of the top government URLs by traffic.

Accessibility Statements (M-24-08)#

OMB Memorandum M-24-08 requires each federal agency to publish a digital accessibility statement that includes contact information for accessibility issues, known limitations, and a link to the agency Section 508 program page.

61 of 100 domains (61%) checked have a detectable accessibility statement at a standard URL path.

Required Federal Links (M-17-06)#

OMB Memorandum M-17-06 and the 21st Century IDEA Act require federal agency public websites to provide links to a Privacy Policy, Contact page, and FOIA page. These were among the compliance criteria benchmarked by the performance.gov federal website performance initiative (no longer active, but the underlying requirements remain in force).

58 of 100 domains (58%) have all three required link types detectable at standard URL paths.

Required federal link detection results for 100 checked domains
Link Type Policy Domains with Link Missing Domains

Section 508: Legal Requirements vs. Best Practices#

Section 508 of the Rehabilitation Act was enacted in 1998 and requires U.S. federal agencies to make their electronic and information technology accessible to people with disabilities. The technical standards were significantly updated through the ICT Accessibility Standards refresh, which became effective in January 2018 — the same year Apple celebrated the 10th anniversary of the iPhone. That refresh incorporated WCAG 2.0 Level AA (published in 2008) as the baseline for web content.

The web has changed dramatically since then. What agencies are legally required to meet today is built on a standard from 2008 — the same era as the original iPhone. Agencies should treat WCAG 2.0 AA as the floor, not the ceiling.

Legal Requirement

WCAG 2.0 Level AA (published 2008)

Referenced by the Section 508 ICT Standards refresh (effective 2018). Agencies must meet these 38 success criteria to satisfy their Section 508 obligations for web content.

  • Text alternatives for non-text content
  • Captions and audio descriptions for multimedia
  • Keyboard accessibility and focus management
  • Minimum color contrast (4.5:1 for normal text)
  • No seizure-inducing content
  • Consistent navigation and labeling
  • Error identification and correction

Section 508 web content requirements (E205)

Best Practices

WCAG 2.1 and 2.2 Level AA (published 2018 and 2023)

These later versions add criteria that reflect how the web actually works today — mobile devices, cognitive accessibility, and modern authentication patterns — and are strongly recommended for any agency aiming to serve all Americans. The US Federal Government is one of the last to use the legacy WCAG 2.0 AA Standard, as it was developed ~2 decades ago.

  • WCAG 2.1 (2018): 17 new criteria for mobile, low vision, and cognitive accessibility
  • WCAG 2.2 (2023): 9 more criteria for authentication and cognitive tasks, removing the outdated "Parsing" criterion
  • W3C now recommends WCAG 2.2 as the current normative standard for new and updated content

What is new in WCAG 2.2 · WCAG 2.2 specification

The scores and findings in this report are based on automated checks aligned with WCAG 2.x rules surfaced by axe-core. Passing automated checks is necessary but not sufficient: manual testing with assistive technologies is required to verify true conformance and to catch barriers that automation cannot detect.

Take Action#

Today's scan identified 231 accessibility barriers across the most-visited U.S. government websites, affecting an estimated 4,810,000 Americans with disabilities. Here is how you can help.

  • Read the federal accessibility report. The 2025 Governmentwide Section 508 Assessment from the GSA Administrator details the state of federal accessibility compliance and the steps agencies are taking to improve.
  • Submit URLs you care about. Want specific government pages included in future scans? Submit them to the Open Scans project to help broaden coverage.
  • Test with free tools. Do your own automated and manual accessibility testing with Accessibility Insights and Google Lighthouse. Automated tools catch a significant portion of WCAG failures quickly; manual review with assistive technologies catches the rest.
  • Adopt and update the U.S. Web Design System (USWDS). The USWDS is already a strong foundation for accessible, consistent federal websites, but many agencies have not adopted it or are running outdated versions. Encourage your agency to adopt or upgrade so every American benefits from its accessibility work.
  • Hire people with disabilities. People with disabilities are the ultimate experts on digital barriers. Bringing them into design, engineering, and testing teams — not just as consultants but as full-time employees — leads to more accessible products and services for everyone.

Accessibility Findings

https://tools.usps.com

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 68

Axe findings: 10

`button`, `link`, and `menuitem` elements do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-command-name)

When an element doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make command elements more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~101KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~101,849 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 10,184,913 affected page loads) ~30KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~30,555 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 10,184,913 affected page loads) ~224KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~224,068 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 10,184,913 affected page loads)

Affected elements (3):

Element 1

Element path: div.panel-heading > h4.panel-title > a#headingOneAnchor > span.down-arr

Snippet:

<span role="button" data-parent="#accordion" aria-expanded="true" aria-controls="collapseOne" class="down-arr">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute

Element 2

Element path: div.panel-heading > h4.panel-title > a.collapsed > span.down-arr

Snippet:

<span role="button" data-parent="#accordion" aria-expanded="true" aria-controls="collapseOne" class="down-arr">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute

Element 3

Element path: div.panel-heading > h4.panel-title > a.collapsed > span.down-arr

Snippet:

<span role="button" data-parent="#accordion" aria-expanded="true" aria-controls="collapseOne" class="down-arr">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Elements with an ARIA `[role]` that require children to contain a specific `[role]` are missing some or all of those required children. (rule: aria-required-children)

Some ARIA parent roles must contain specific child roles to perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about roles and required children elements.

Some ARIA child roles must be contained by specific parent roles to properly perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about ARIA roles and required parent element.

`[aria-*]` attributes do not have valid values (rule: aria-valid-attr-value)

Assistive technologies, like screen readers, can't interpret ARIA attributes with invalid values. Learn more about valid values for ARIA attributes.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Label: What does my tracking number look like?

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Element 1

Element path: div#tracking_page_wrapper > div#g-navigation > div.nav-full > a#endnav

Snippet:

<a name="endnav" id="endnav" href="#" class="hidden-skip">

Label:  

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Element is in tab order and does not have accessible text
  • Fix any of the following:
  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute

Element 2

Element path: body.tracking_reskin > div#global-footer--wrap > footer.global-footer > a.global-footer--logo-link

Snippet:

<a href="https://www.usps.com/" class="global-footer--logo-link">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Element is in tab order and does not have accessible text
  • Fix any of the following:
  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

https://usps.com

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr)

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Estimated ~19,954 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 1,995,437 affected page loads) ~5KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~5,986 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 1,995,437 affected page loads) ~43KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Affected elements (4):

Element 1

Element path: div#usps-carousel > div.carousel-inner > ol.carousel-indicators > li.active

Snippet:

<li data-target="#usps-carousel" data-slide-to="0" aria-checked="true" class="active">

Label: Slide 1

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-checked="true"

Element 2

Element path: div#usps-carousel > div.carousel-inner > ol.carousel-indicators > li

Snippet:

<li data-target="#usps-carousel" data-slide-to="1" aria-checked="false">

Label: Slide 2

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-checked="false"

Element 3

Element path: div#usps-carousel > div.carousel-inner > ol.carousel-indicators > li

Snippet:

<li data-target="#usps-carousel" data-slide-to="2" aria-checked="false">

Label: Slide 3

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-checked="false"

Element 4

Element path: div#usps-carousel > div.carousel-inner > ol.carousel-indicators > li

Snippet:

<li data-target="#usps-carousel" data-slide-to="3" aria-checked="false">

Label: Slide 4

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-checked="false"
Elements with an ARIA `[role]` that require children to contain a specific `[role]` are missing some or all of those required children. (rule: aria-required-children)

Some ARIA parent roles must contain specific child roles to perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about roles and required children elements.

`[role]`s are not contained by their required parent element (rule: aria-required-parent)

Some ARIA child roles must be contained by specific parent roles to properly perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about ARIA roles and required parent element.

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty

  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~47KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~47,890 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 1,995,437 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0, maximum-scale=1.0, user-scalable=no">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices
Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main)

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Disabilities affected: ~19KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Download full axe findings JSON | Download full axe findings CSV

Accessibility Findings

https://forecast.weather.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 67

Axe findings: 5

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~40KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~40,338 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 1,680,736 affected page loads) ~72KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~72,272 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 1,680,736 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.one-sixth-first > div#forecast-lookup > form#getForecast > input#inputstring

Snippet:

<input id="inputstring" name="inputstring" type="text" value="Enter location ..." onclick="this.value=''" autocomplete="off">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 2.84 (foreground color: #999999, background color: #ffffff, font size: 8.0pt (10.6667px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~16KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~16,807 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 1,680,736 affected page loads) ~5KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~5,042 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 1,680,736 affected page loads) ~78KPeople with cognitive, learning, or language differences. Estimated ~78,995 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 1,680,736 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute
Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~12KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~12,678 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 1,267,820 affected page loads) ~3KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~3,803 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 1,267,820 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div > div.blazy > div.media > img.media__element

Snippet:

<img decoding="async" class="media__element b-lazy" loading="lazy" src="" width="480" height="360">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Elements with visible text labels do not have matching accessible names. (rule: label-content-name-mismatch)

Visible text labels that do not match the accessible name can result in a confusing experience for screen reader users. Learn more about accessible names.

Estimated ~27,892 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 1,267,820 affected page loads)

Accessibility Findings

https://secure.ssa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 90

Axe findings: 4

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Estimated ~24,968 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 1,040,348 affected page loads) ~44KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~44,735 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 1,040,348 affected page loads)

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Accessibility Findings

https://studentaid.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 91

Axe findings: 3

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Disabilities affected: ~8KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~8,883 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 888,312 affected page loads) ~2KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~2,665 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 888,312 affected page loads) ~19KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~19,543 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 888,312 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.home-banner > div.row > div.col-md-5 > h4#fsa_Title_PopularTasks

Snippet:

<h4 _ngcontent-dxu-c154="" id="fsa_Title_PopularTasks" class="text-left fsa-mb-px-16 fsa-color-white">

Label: POPULAR TOPICS

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Heading order invalid
Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~21KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~21,319 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 888,312 affected page loads) ~38KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~38,197 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 888,312 affected page loads)

Affected elements (2):

Element 1

Element path: div.fsa-banner-content-body > span > div > a

Snippet:

<a href="/announcements-events/idr-court-actions">

Label: visit StudentAid.gov/courtactions

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.64:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #0000ee, surrounding text: #212529)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Element 2

Element path: div.fsa-banner-content-body > span > div > a

Snippet:

<a href="/fsa-id/sign-in/landing/?redirectTo=/my-aid">

Label: Log in

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.64:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #0000ee, surrounding text: #212529)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text
Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~19KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~19,543 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 888,312 affected page loads) ~51KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~51,522 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 888,312 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: footer#fsa-universal-footer > section.fsa-top-links > a#fsa_Link_FooterLevel1SiteSurvey

Snippet:

<a class="fsa-footer-link fsa-delimited-link-mobile fsa-top-footer-links bold" id="fsa_Link_FooterLevel1SiteSurvey" href="javascript:window.KAMPYLE_ONSITE_SDK.showForm('308');">

Label: Site Survey

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (61px by 23px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 23px instead of at least 24px.

Accessibility Findings

https://cdc.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 91

Axe findings: 3

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~8KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~8,247 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 824,661 affected page loads) ~2KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~2,474 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 824,661 affected page loads) ~18KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~18,143 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 824,661 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: nav.mobile-navbar > div.mobile-navbar_top > div.d-flex > a#cdc-mobile-search-toggle

Snippet:

<a id="cdc-mobile-search-toggle" class="nav-link" href="#" aria-controls="cdc-mobile-search-form">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Element is in tab order and does not have accessible text
  • Fix any of the following:
  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Skip links are not focusable. (rule: skip-link)

Including a skip link can help users skip to the main content to save time. Learn more about skip links.

Disabilities affected: ~8KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~8,247 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 824,661 affected page loads) ~2KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~2,474 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 824,661 affected page loads) ~18KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~18,143 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 824,661 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: body.cdc-post-type--cdc-homepage > div#skipmenu > a.skippy

Snippet:

<a class="skippy visually-hidden-focusable" href="#headerSearch" tabindex="2">

Label: Skip to search

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • No skip link target
Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~8KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Download full axe findings JSON | Download full axe findings CSV

Accessibility Findings

https://travel.state.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 93

Axe findings: 2

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~17KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~17,954 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 748,091 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0, maximum-scale=1.0, user-scalable=no">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices
Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main)

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Disabilities affected: ~7KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~7,481 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 748,091 affected page loads) ~2KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~16,458 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 748,091 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html lang="en">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Document does not have a main landmark

Accessibility Findings

https://weather.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 67

Axe findings: 5

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~15KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~15,392 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 641,318 affected page loads) ~27KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~27,577 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 641,318 affected page loads)

Element 1

Element path: div.one-sixth-first > div#forecast-lookup > form#getForecast > input#inputstring

Snippet:

<input id="inputstring" name="inputstring" type="text" value="Enter location ..." onclick="this.value=''" autocomplete="off">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 2.84 (foreground color: #999999, background color: #ffffff, font size: 8.0pt (10.6667px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~6KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~6,413 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 641,318 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~30,142 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 641,318 affected page loads)

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Lists do not contain only `<li>` elements and script supporting elements (`<script>` and `<template>`). (rule: list)

Screen readers have a specific way of announcing lists. Ensuring proper list structure aids screen reader output. Learn more about proper list structure.

Disabilities affected: ~5KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~5,837 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 583,710 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,751 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 583,710 affected page loads)

Element 1

Element path: div > div.most-wanted-view > div.item-list > ul.slick-initialized

Snippet:

<ul class="slick-initialized slick-slider slick-dotted">

Label: Oscar Noe Medina Gonzalez DEA Field Office: New York Southern Wanted For: : CON…

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • List element has direct children that are not allowed: div

Accessibility Findings

https://tsa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 99

Axe findings: 1

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Estimated ~12,186 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 553,894 affected page loads)

Accessibility Findings

https://urs.earthdata.nasa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 81

Axe findings: 5

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~4,668 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 466,758 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,400 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 466,758 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div#tophat3Widget > nav#tophat3WidgetNavBar > div.container-fluid > button#tophat3HamburgerButton

Snippet:

<button class="navbar-toggler" type="button" id="tophat3HamburgerButton">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~11KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~11,202 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 466,758 affected page loads) ~20KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~20,071 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 466,758 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: section#callout-login > form#login > p.button-with-notes > input.eui-btn--round

Snippet:

<input type="submit" name="commit" value="Log in" class="eui-btn--round eui-btn--green" data-disable-with="Log in">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 2.07 (foreground color: #ffffff, background color: #20ce6f, font size: 12.0pt (16px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~4,668 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 466,758 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,400 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 466,758 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: nav#tophat3WidgetNavBar > div.container-fluid > a#tophat3-app-title-link > img#tophat3-nasa-meatball

Snippet:

<img id="tophat3-nasa-meatball" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PD94bWwgdmVyc2lvbj0iMS4wIiBlbmNvZGluZz0iVVRGLTgi…">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~11KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~11,202 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 466,758 affected page loads) ~20KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~20,071 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 466,758 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: body.static_pages > div.container > div.home-info-box > a

Snippet:

<a href="https://earthdata.nasa.gov/about/daacs">

Label: DAACs

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 2.59:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #2276ac, surrounding text: #323232)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text
Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main)

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~4,668 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 466,758 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,400 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 466,758 affected page loads) ~10KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~10,269 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 466,758 affected page loads)

Element 1

Element path: html.js

Snippet:

<html lang="en" class=" js flexbox canvas canvastext webgl touch geolocation postmessage no-websq…">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Document does not have a main landmark

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Axe findings: 1

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~10KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~10,187 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 463,043 affected page loads) ~26KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~26,856 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 463,043 affected page loads)

Affected elements (15):

Element 1

Element path: div.row > div.col-xs-12 > sup > a#password-tip

Snippet:

<a role="button" id="password-tip" tabindex="0" data-placement="top" data-remotecontent="/entreg/assets/html/tooltips/tool-tip-forgot-useralias.html" data-toggle="popover" aria-label="Retrieve your username" data-bs-original-title="Retrieve your username">

Label: Retrieve your username

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (12px by 15px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 21px instead of at least 24px.

Element path: div.row > div.col-xs-12 > sup > a#password-tip

<a role="button" id="password-tip" tabindex="0" data-placement="top" data-remotecontent="/entreg/assets/html/tooltips/tool-tip-forgot-password.html" data-toggle="popover" aria-label="It's easy to create a new one" data-bs-original-title="It's easy to create a new one">

Accessibility Findings

https://egov.uscis.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 90

Axe findings: 1

The document uses `<meta http-equiv="refresh">` (rule: meta-refresh)

Users do not expect a page to refresh automatically, and doing so will move focus back to the top of the page. This may create a frustrating or confusing experience. Learn more about the refresh meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~4,463 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 446,275 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,339 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 446,275 affected page loads) ~9KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~9,818 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 446,275 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta http-equiv="refresh" content="360">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • <meta> tag forces timed refresh of page (less than 20 hours)

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Element 1

Snippet:

Label: NOTICE: CBP Home Stipend Increase

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Heading order invalid
Elements with visible text labels do not have matching accessible names. (rule: label-content-name-mismatch)

Visible text labels that do not match the accessible name can result in a confusing experience for screen reader users. Learn more about accessible names.

Download full axe findings JSON | Download full axe findings CSV

Accessibility Findings

https://nasa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 83

Axe findings: 5

Elements with `role="dialog"` or `role="alertdialog"` do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-dialog-name)

ARIA dialog elements without accessible names may prevent screen readers users from discerning the purpose of these elements. Learn how to make ARIA dialog elements more accessible.

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: body.home > div#pum-872473

Snippet:

<div id="pum-872473" role="dialog" aria-modal="false" class="pum pum-overlay pum-theme-862814 pum-theme-content-only popmake-overlay pu…" data-popmake="{&quot;id&quot;:872473,&quot;slug&quot;:&quot;was-this-page-helpful&quot;,&quot;theme_id&quot;:862814,&quot;cookies&quot;:[{…">

Label: Was this page helpful? Was this page helpful? Yes No ×

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

<a href="https://www.nasa.gov/accessibility/">

https://science.nasa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 95

Axe findings: 1

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Estimated ~3,100 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 309,963 affected page loads) ~930People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content.   • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty

  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.5:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #d6d7d9, surrounding text: #aeb0b5)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Accessibility Findings

https://my.uscis.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 89

Axe findings: 2

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr)

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,668 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 266,764 affected page loads) ~800People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~800 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 266,764 affected page loads) ~5KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~5,869 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 266,764 affected page loads)

Element 1

Element path: div.uscis-row > div.col-span-6 > div.mobile-header > a#toggle-sections

Snippet:

<a class="hamburger hamburger--squeeze" tabindex="0" type="button" id="toggle-sections" aria-expanded="false">

Label: Menu

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • ARIA attribute is not allowed: aria-expanded="false"
`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~6KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~6,402 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 266,764 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,height=device-height,user-scalable=no,initial-scale=1.0…">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

https://esta.cbp.dhs.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 85

Axe findings: 4

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Lists do not contain only `<li>` elements and script supporting elements (`<script>` and `<template>`). (rule: list)

Screen readers have a specific way of announcing lists. Ensuring proper list structure aids screen reader output. Learn more about proper list structure.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,198 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 219,815 affected page loads) ~659People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main)

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Download full axe findings JSON | Download full axe findings CSV

Accessibility Findings

https://radar.weather.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 77

Axe findings: 2

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,180 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 217,997 affected page loads) ~654People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~654 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 217,997 affected page loads) ~10KPeople with cognitive, learning, or language differences. Estimated ~10,246 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 217,997 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html>

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute
`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~5KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~5,232 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 217,997 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1, maximum-scale=1, user-scalable=0">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

Accessibility Findings

https://fas.usda.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Axe findings: 2

`[aria-hidden="true"]` elements contain focusable descendents (rule: aria-hidden-focus)

Focusable descendents within an `[aria-hidden="true"]` element prevent those interactive elements from being available to users of assistive technologies like screen readers. Learn how `aria-hidden` affects focusable elements.

Estimated ~4,772 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 216,893 affected page loads)

Elements with visible text labels do not have matching accessible names. (rule: label-content-name-mismatch)

Accessibility Findings

https://home.treasury.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Axe findings: 1

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~4,693 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 213,323 affected page loads) ~12KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~12,373 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 213,323 affected page loads)

Affected elements (2):

Element 1

Element path: div.site-menu > ul > li > a

Snippet:

<a href="https://home.treasury.gov/subfooter/google-privacy-policy">

Label: Google Privacy

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (85.3px by 18px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 20.6px instead of at least 24px.

Element 2

Element path: div.site-menu > ul > li > a

Snippet:

<a href="/about/careers-at-treasury" data-display-mobile="0" data-display-desktop="0" data-drupal-link-system-path="node/4341">

Label: Careers

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (43.6px by 18px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 20.6px instead of at least 24px.

Elements with an ARIA `[role]` that require children to contain a specific `[role]` are missing some or all of those required children. (rule: aria-required-children)

Some ARIA parent roles must contain specific child roles to perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about roles and required children elements.

Estimated ~2,000 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 199,955 affected page loads) ~600People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. `[role]`s are not contained by their required parent element (rule: aria-required-parent)

Some ARIA child roles must be contained by specific parent roles to properly perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about ARIA roles and required parent element.

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~4,799 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 199,955 affected page loads) ~8KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~8,598 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 199,955 affected page loads)

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

https://sam.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Axe findings: 2

Elements use prohibited ARIA attributes (rule: aria-prohibited-attr)

Using ARIA attributes in roles where they are prohibited can mean that important information is not communicated to users of assistive technologies. Learn more about prohibited ARIA roles.

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.paragraph__column > div.px-video-container > div#progress-bar > div#progress

Snippet:

<div class="progress" id="progress" aria-label="Video: " style="width: 0%;">

Label: Video:

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • aria-label attribute cannot be used on a div with no valid role attribute.
Elements with visible text labels do not have matching accessible names. (rule: label-content-name-mismatch)

Visible text labels that do not match the accessible name can result in a confusing experience for screen reader users. Learn more about accessible names.

https://earthquake.usgs.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 94

Axe findings: 2

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Estimated ~8,304 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 176,672 affected page loads)

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 85

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,714 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 171,429 affected page loads) ~514People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Element path: div.col-12 > div.official-banner > div#accordion > img.ues-usa-img

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~4,114 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 171,429 affected page loads) ~7KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~7,371 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 171,429 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.col-12 > div.official-banner > div#accordion > a#banner-toggle

Snippet:

<a id="banner-toggle" data-toggle="collapse" href="#" data-target="#collapseOne" aria-expanded="true">

Label: Here's how you know.

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 2.01:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #0f68b8, surrounding text: #373a3c)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text
`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~4,114 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 171,429 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, user-scalable=no">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr)

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Axe findings: 5

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~3,550 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 147,917 affected page loads) ~6KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~6,360 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 147,917 affected page loads)

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,479 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 147,917 affected page loads) ~444People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~444 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 147,917 affected page loads) ~6KPeople with cognitive, learning, or language differences. Estimated ~6,952 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 147,917 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html>

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute
Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,479 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 147,917 affected page loads) ~444People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Element 1

Element path: tbody > tr > td > a.ver12MainLayout

Snippet:

<a class="ver12MainLayout" href="/npspub/pub_cmn_login/index_live.jsp">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Element is in tab order and does not have accessible text
  • Fix any of the following:
  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
